evals.prompt: I'm building a SaaS platform where agents make outbound calls to customers. Right now everything lives in a big `Calls` context — there's a Call schema with fields for room info, callee details, billing duration, and task assignment. It's becoming a mess. How should I think about breaking this up?
evals.expected_output: Identifies 'Call' as overloaded, suggests separate bounded contexts per subdomain, shows API examples with scope parameter, uses domain language names

assertions.name: identifies-call-as-overloaded
assertions.description: Identifies that 'Call' means different things in different subdomains (telephony, billing, task management)

assertions.name: suggests-separate-contexts
assertions.description: Suggests at least 2-3 separate bounded contexts (e.g., BackgroundCalls, Billing, TaskManagement)

assertions.name: shows-scope-parameter
assertions.description: Shows scope as first parameter in at least one command/query example

assertions.name: uses-domain-language-names
assertions.description: Context names use domain language (not generic names like 'CallsBilling' or 'CallsTasks')

assertions.name: avoids-proactive-refactor-push
assertions.description: Works with the user's existing structure or asks their intent rather than demanding a full refactor

evals.prompt: We're building a checkout flow for our B2B SaaS. When a customer places an order: we need to capture payment, reserve stock, and send a confirmation email. All of this currently happens in one giant function in our Orders context. How should we model the contexts and coordinate between them?
evals.expected_output: Identifies 4 bounded contexts, shows commands returning events, use case with with pipeline, scope on commands, partial failure mention

assertions.name: identifies-four-contexts
assertions.description: Identifies Checkout, Billing, Fulfillment, and Notifications (or equivalent domain-language names) as separate contexts

assertions.name: commands-return-events
assertions.description: Shows commands returning domain events (e.g., place_order returns {:ok, %OrderPlaced{}})

assertions.name: shows-use-case-with-pipeline
assertions.description: Shows a use case module with a with pipeline coordinating the contexts

assertions.name: enforce-keys-on-events
assertions.description: Event structs use @enforce_keys

assertions.name: scope-on-commands
assertions.description: All commands shown include scope as first parameter

assertions.name: mentions-partial-failure-risk
assertions.description: Mentions the partial failure risk (payment captured before stock reserved) without over-prescribing a solution

assertions.name: no-internal-schema-in-events
assertions.description: Events contain primitive fields only (IDs, values), not internal schema structs from another context

evals.prompt: Should I create a context called `Users`? I need to handle: login/authentication, user profile editing, subscription plan management, and workspace member roles. I also have admins who manage other users.
evals.expected_output: Challenges Users catch-all, identifies at least 3 subdomains, suggests domain-language names, explains separate models per context

assertions.name: challenges-users-catch-all
assertions.description: Challenges the 'Users' catch-all context rather than just accepting it as given

assertions.name: identifies-separate-subdomains
assertions.description: Identifies at least 3 distinct subdomains (auth/identity, billing/subscriptions, workspace/membership)

assertions.name: suggests-domain-language-names
assertions.description: Suggests domain-language names like Identity, Subscriptions, Workspace rather than UsersAuth, UsersBilling, etc.

assertions.name: different-user-model-per-context
assertions.description: Explains that each context has its own model of 'user' with only the fields relevant to that context

assertions.name: collects-context-before-reframing
assertions.description: Does not immediately prescribe a full architecture — shows awareness of user's existing situation

evals.prompt: I have a feature where when an agent joins a room, we need to: start their dialing session in BackgroundCalls, update their task status in TaskManagement, and log the event for audit. BackgroundCalls currently calls TaskManagement directly inside its start_dialing_session function. Is this the right approach?
evals.expected_output: Flags cross-context coupling as anti-pattern, shows BackgroundCalls emitting event, use case coordinating both, audit as event subscriber

assertions.name: identifies-antipattern
assertions.description: Identifies BackgroundCalls calling TaskManagement directly as a cross-context coupling anti-pattern

assertions.name: suggests-event-based-decoupling
assertions.description: Suggests BackgroundCalls emits a DialingSessionStarted (or similar) event instead of calling TaskManagement

assertions.name: shows-use-case-coordination
assertions.description: Shows a use case module coordinating BackgroundCalls and TaskManagement via the event

assertions.name: audit-as-event-subscriber
assertions.description: Suggests audit logging as an event subscriber pattern rather than a direct call

assertions.name: shows-event-struct
assertions.description: Shows the domain event struct (DialingSessionStarted or equivalent) with enforce_keys or fields
